pathophysiology_summary: Polymyalgia rheumatica is an inflammatory condition of adults over 50 causing bilateral, symmetric proximal (shoulder and hip girdle) pain and prolonged stiffness, worse with rest and cold, with borderline-to-raised ESR/CRP and a dramatic response to low-dose corticosteroids. The acute immobilising girdle stiffness mimics a neurological or orthopaedic crisis, but PMR does not affect distal neurology or bone structure. The exclusions are cervical myelopathy/cord compression (distal weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction — screenable by history), occult proximal fracture (unilateral, mechanism, point tenderness) and, critically, giant cell arteritis, which overlaps PMR and threatens vision (temporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual symptoms). Management is interim analgesia and warmth with urgent GP review to start low-dose steroids, plus a firm GCA safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetric proximal girdle stiffness worse with rest/cold in an older adult.
Screen distal power/sensation and bladder/bowel; absent here.
PMR overlaps GCA — screen temporal headache/scalp tenderness/jaw claudication/visual symptoms.
No trauma, bilateral symmetric, no point tenderness.
Intact distal neurology (DI-001), no GCA features (DI-002), bilateral symmetric proximal stiffness worse with rest/cold, no trauma (DI-003) — a PMR flare.
Normal distal power/sensation and no bladder/bowel dysfunction (safety-net retained).
No current features, but GCA can emerge in PMR — retained as the escalation trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: reassure a PMR flare, interim analgesia (NSAID if tolerated) and warmth, urgent next-day GP review to commence low-dose steroids, and a firm GCA safety-net (visual/temporal-headache/jaw-claudication -> emergency). Ambulance over-triage for a symmetric proximal flare with intact neurology is unnecessary.

red_flags.description: Cervical myelopathy/cord compression — distal weakness, sensory level, or bladder/bowel dysfunction
Giant cell arteritis — temporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or visual symptoms

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a PMR flare without screening or safety-netting GCA, missing an emerging sight-threatening arteritis.
Escalating a symmetric proximal flare with intact distal neurology to an ambulance for presumed cord compression.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Interim analgesia + warmth + urgent next-day GP review to start low-dose steroids; firm GCA safety-net; emergency escalation for myelopathy/GCA features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring without the GCA/myelopathy screen could miss an emergency; over-triaging a symmetric proximal flare wastes resources.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: PMR flare with no current GCA features
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Up to a fifth of PMR patients develop GCA; maintain a firm GCA safety-net and low threshold to escalate for cranial/visual symptoms.
